title: Chocolate on Cotton
stainChemistry: Chocolate is a three-component stain: cocoa butter (triglyceride oil, ~50% of cocoa solids mass) that wicks into cotton fiber; milk proteins (casein + whey) that denature under heat and act as adhesive for the other components; and cocoa solids — phenolic tannins + theobromine that produce the brown color. Cotton is pure cellulose: chemically durable, tolerates enzymes, NSD emulsification, and 3% H₂O₂ oxidation at room temperature. The only hard constraint is heat before the protein is cleared — hot water sets the casein into the fiber and the whole stain matrix with it.
whyThisWorks: Three components, three chemistries, one ordering rule: protein before heat, oil before pigment. Start mechanical (scrape hardened cocoa butter off). Enzyme digests the casein matrix that binds the rest. NSD emulsifies the dispersed cocoa butter so it rinses out. H₂O₂ oxidizes the residual phenolic tannin to colorless. Hot wash at the end is safe — by then the casein is gone and can't set.
defaultAssumption: Fresh chocolate on cotton is a high-confidence DIY. Set chocolate (hours old, ironed, or washed hot without enzyme first) climbs a tier but stays in home scope. Dark cotton narrows the peroxide lane.

spottingProtocol.technique: Harden, scrape, flush from the back
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade / spoon, cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape any solid chocolate off with a dull blade. If the stain's still soft, chill the fabric briefly to harden the cocoa butter first — brittle chocolate pops off cleanly; soft chocolate smears. Then cold-flush from the BACK of the fabric. Cold only — any warm or hot water at this stage sets the milk protein into the fiber.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease digestant) or enzyme pre-soak dissolved in warm water (30–38°C)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply the Protein Spotter directly to the stain or fully submerge in a pre-soak solution. Enzyme window is warm, not hot — protease denatures above 50°C. Dwell 20–30 min, brush gently with a soft bristle brush after the first 10 minutes. The casein matrix is what holds the rest of the chocolate in the fiber; once digested, the oil and pigment lift in the next two steps.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush in small circles. NSD emulsifies the residual cocoa butter the enzyme freed from the fiber. Rinse cool from the back.

For the residual brown tannin shadow: apply 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Peroxide oxidizes the phenolic/theobromine color to colorless. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. Repeat once if faint shadow persists. One oxidizer at a time.

Rinse cool until water runs clear. Launder on the hottest setting the care label permits — by this point the casein is gone and hot wash won't set anything. Hot water clears any residual chemistry and finishes the job.

steps: 1. Scrape + cold flush from the back. Harden soft chocolate before scraping.
2. Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease) in warm water (30–38°C). Dwell 20–30 min.
3. NSD to emulsify residual cocoa butter. Light brush.
4. 3% H₂O₂ on residual tannin shadow. Dwell 3–5 min. Repeat once if needed.
5. Cool rinse + hot wash per care label.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or protease digestant)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold flush; warm (30–38°C) for enzyme; cool through NSD + peroxide; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never warm-water-flush fresh chocolate — sets the casein
Enzyme window is warm, not hot — above 50°C the protease dies
One oxidizer at a time — NSD-flushed between if a second pass is needed
Flush all chemistry before heat — residual oxidizer yellows cotton

homeSolutions.instruction: Get the solid chocolate off before any water touches the stain. A dull butter knife or the edge of a credit card works. If the chocolate's still soft, stick the fabric in the freezer for 15 minutes first — hardened chocolate pops off cleanly, smearing is what drives the stain deeper.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Flush From the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for a minute. Let the water push the chocolate out the way it came in. Never use warm or hot water here — the milk protein in chocolate cooks into cotton if you do.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat — Warm Water, Not Hot
homeSolutions.instruction: Use an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout). Dissolve in WARM water per the label — hot water kills the enzymes. Apply to the stain, let it sit 20–30 minutes. The enzymes digest the milk proteins that hold everything else in the fabric.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Soft Brush for the Oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Add a drop of Dawn dish soap to the spot and work it in gently with a soft toothbrush or a fingertip. Dawn breaks up the cocoa butter the enzyme freed. Rinse cool.

If a brown shadow remains after the soap pass, dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot. Let it bubble for 3–5 minutes, blot with a clean white towel. Skip this step on dark or dyed cotton; test an inside seam first if unsure.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fume risk.

homeSolutions.instruction: Rinse with cool water from the back until the towel runs clean. Then launder on the hottest setting the care label allows — by now the protein is gone and hot water is safe. Inspect in daylight BEFORE the dryer. If you still see the stain, repeat the enzyme + peroxide pass first.

If the chocolate's already been through a hot dryer, is older than 48 hours, or survives two full home rounds, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Chocolate on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with enzyme pretreat + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide. No heat until after the enzyme."

steps: 1. Scrape solid chocolate off first — freeze the fabric if soft. Don't smear.
2. Cold water flush from the BACK of the fabric — never warm.
3. Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout) dissolved in WARM water. Dwell 20–30 min.
4. Dawn dish soap + soft brush to lift the cocoa butter.
5. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual brown shadow. Skip on dark cotton.
7. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the chocolate has been through a hot dryer, if two home rounds haven't cleared it, or if the cotton is dark dyed and the peroxide step isn't safe. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: Never warm-flush chocolate while fresh — cooks the milk protein into the fiber
Enzymes die above 50°C — warm water for the pretreat, not hot
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
Skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton or inside-seam-test first

neverDo: Never apply hot water while milk protein is still in the fiber — cooks the stain in
Never run enzymes above 50°C — denatures the protease
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, flushed between
Never rub wet chocolate — smearing is worse than leaving it alone
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates enzymes, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash after chemistry is cleared
Dyed cotton: test an inside seam before peroxide
Cotton is heat-durable once the protein is gone — hot wash is the finisher

materialWarnings: Heat sets milk protein — cold flush only while the stain is fresh
Enzymes work in warm water, not hot
Peroxide on whites; test inside seam on dark cotton

escalation.when: Chocolate already heat-set by dryer or iron, two home rounds failed, or dark dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe.
escalation.whatToTell: "Chocolate on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, older?
Has it been through a dryer or iron since?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Milk chocolate, dark chocolate, or chocolate sauce? (more milk = more protein; more dark = more tannin)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Protein-first ordering is non-negotiable — hot water before the enzyme cooks the casein and locks every other component in. Fresh + white = high-confidence DIY. Hot-set = pro.
